title: With Putin visibly weakened, now is the time to back Ukraine
gist: Ukraine's deep-strike campaign has sparked a Russian fuel crisis, imposed a drone 'logistics lockdown' on Crimea, and left Putin more vulnerable than at any time since 2022; the author argues Western allies should exploit this window while warning Putin may respond with mobilization or a Baltic provocation.

insights.claim: Putin launched the 2022 invasion expecting a quick, complete victory to erase the Soviet collapse and revive Russia's great power status, a bet that now looks like one of the most disastrous miscalculations in modern history.
insights.speaker: Peter Dickinson
insights.so_what: Framing the war as a strategic blunder underpins the view that time favors Ukraine, informing the risk that Russia escalates rather than concedes.
insights.locator: When Russian President Vladimir Putin launched

insights.claim: Since the start of 2026, escalating Ukrainian missile and drone strikes on military and industrial targets inside Russia have hampered the Kremlin war machine and sparked a fuel crisis threatening to destabilize Putin's home front.
insights.speaker: Peter Dickinson
insights.so_what: Deep strikes on Russian energy and industry create domestic fuel scarcity and product-market tightness; a template for asymmetric strategic effect.

insights.claim: Ukraine imposed a 'logistics lockdown' of occupied Crimea by using drones to target all major supply routes, forcing occupation authorities to restrict fuel sales to civilians and declare a state of emergency.
insights.speaker: Peter Dickinson
insights.so_what: Cheap drones can throttle logistics to a strategically vital region, demonstrating a low-cost interdiction capability with broad military implications.
insights.locator: The current situation in Russian-occupied Crimea

insights.claim: Ukraine marked the opening of Putin's St. Petersburg International Economic Forum in early June by bombing the city's oil terminal and naval base, embarrassing the Kremlin before visiting foreign delegations.
insights.speaker: Peter Dickinson
insights.so_what: High-visibility strikes on Russian energy assets during flagship events damage Russia's investment image and signal Ukrainian reach.
insights.locator: In early June, Ukraine marked the opening

insights.claim: Ukraine's strikes are challenging the unspoken social contract in which Putin promised Russians higher living standards and protection from his wars in exchange for political passivity, an agreement now looking outdated as cities are bombed and millions queue for fuel.
insights.speaker: Peter Dickinson
insights.so_what: Domestic hardship reaching ordinary Russians raises the political cost of the war for Putin, a slow-building pressure on Kremlin stability.

insights.claim: Russia's army struggled to advance at all in 2026 with minor gains canceled by Ukrainian counteroffensives, despite heavy casualties that the UK estimated in late May at around half a million Russian soldiers lost since the invasion began.
insights.speaker: Peter Dickinson
insights.so_what: Stalled advances plus ~500,000 casualties quantify the diminishing returns on Russia's offensive, reinforcing that attrition favors Ukraine's negotiating position.

insights.claim: Since the start of the year Russia has lost key allies in Venezuela and Hungary, while efforts to bully Armenia proved counterproductive and undermined Russian influence in the southern Caucasus.
insights.speaker: Peter Dickinson
insights.so_what: Erosion of Russia's alliance network signals diminished global leverage, relevant to sanctions durability and geopolitical realignment.
insights.locator: Putin has also been confronted with a series

insights.claim: Contrary to Kremlin hopes after Trump's 2024 election win, Europe dramatically stepped up military aid to Kyiv, purchasing US weapons and significantly increasing direct funding for the Ukrainian war effort.
insights.speaker: Peter Dickinson
insights.so_what: Rising European funding and US weapons purchases sustain demand for Western defense output even as US posture wavers; the aid-collapse thesis keeps failing.
insights.locator: Meanwhile, the Kremlin's efforts to undermine

insights.claim: Putin has responded to declining fortunes by clamping down on domestic internet access inside Russia and escalating attacks on Ukraine's civilian population.
insights.speaker: Peter Dickinson
insights.so_what: Internet clampdown and civilian escalation are signs of regime insecurity, not strength, and raise humanitarian and escalation risk.
insights.locator: On the contrary, Putin has reacted

insights.claim: Speculation is mounting that Moscow may be preparing a limited military operation in the Baltic region to raise the stakes and intimidate Europe into abandoning support for Ukraine.
insights.speaker: Peter Dickinson
insights.so_what: A Baltic provocation is a live NATO Article 5 tail risk that would sharply reprice European security and defense assets.

insights.claim: Putin is deeply reluctant to order a politically risky mass mobilization but will almost certainly do so rather than admit defeat, and it is unlikely to spark major unrest given the absence of any real anti-war movement or opposition in Russia.
insights.speaker: Peter Dickinson
insights.so_what: Mobilization is the probable path to sustain Russian manpower; it prolongs the war rather than ending it, keeping defense demand elevated.
insights.locator: Nor is Putin likely to run out of soldiers

insights.claim: The author argues eighteen months of failed compromise negotiations confirm Putin will not stop until he is stopped, and Western leaders have a window to secure peace through strength by boosting support for Ukraine.
insights.speaker: Peter Dickinson
insights.so_what: The 'peace through strength' framing implies continued and possibly increased Western military spending rather than a near-term negotiated settlement.
insights.locator: At the same time, the past eighteen months

insights.claim: Ukraine's priority requests are expanded reliable Patriot interceptor supply, more Western missiles to strike deep inside Russia, investment in Ukraine's innovative but underfunded defense industry, and maintained sanctions that close Kremlin circumvention loopholes.
insights.speaker: Peter Dickinson
insights.so_what: The wish list flags sustained demand for interceptors, long-range munitions, and Ukrainian defense-industry investment as procurement themes.
insights.locator: Ukraine's wish list is well known
